coredata.dc:title: Hybrid NiO nano rods-quantum carbon dots composites as a novel corrosion protective coating on EN3 mild steel in the marine environment

coredata.dc:description: This research endeavors to synthesize nickel oxide nanoparticles (NiO NPs) using a sonochemical process facilitated by a nitrate precursor and alkaline sodium hydroxide (NaOH). In parallel, carbon dots (CDs) were derived from lemon waste peels via a facile hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the reflux method fabricated nickel oxide- carbon dot nanocomposite (NiO/CDs) in a 5:1 ratio. The structural properties, including crystallography, crystallinity, particle size, and morphology, were comprehensively characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The synthesized nanoparticles exhibit excellent dispersion, with mean particle sizes of approximately 36.46 nm for NiO NPs and 2.17 nm for carbon dots. The carbon dots defect ratio (ID/IG) characteristics feature was estimated as 0.94 using Raman spectroscopy. NiO, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating were developed using a doctor blade method on the EN3 mild steel specimens. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was conducted in marine water conditions with 3.5 wt% of NaCl and seawater to evaluate the stability of the nanostructured coating. Electrochemical analysis, such as the Nyquist plot, Tafel plot, and frequency response finding, demonstrated that NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating has enhanced corrosion protection in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution compares to seawater medium. These findings underscore the potential of the NiO/CDs nanostructured coating to extend the lifespan of industrial mild steel (EN3) specimens in seawater environments.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: A. Karthik received his Ph.D. in Technology (Nano) from Anna University, Chennai. He holds an M. Tech in Nano Science and Technology, as well as an M. Sc and B. Sc in Physics. He is a recipient of the CSIR Senior Research Fellowship (SRF), Delhi. Additionally, he has 12 years of research experience, including 8 years of teaching at K.S. Rangasamy College of Technology, Tamil Nadu, India. He has also served as a guest faculty member at the Central University of Pondicherry and the Central University of Tamil Nadu. He is actively involved in the mass production of various nanometal oxides, including green catalytic materials, for diverse societal applications such as wastewater and greywater treatment, environmental corrosion protection, thermal barrier coatings, electrode materials for supercapacitors, and biomaterials for tissue engineering.

One of the authors, S. Savitha, acknowledges the financial support from the Tamil Nadu State Council for Science and Technology (TNSCST) through the RFRS scheme, TNSCST/RFRS/05/VM/2022-23/16395 (2087) dated 17.10.2024. The authors also acknowledge the DST-FIST [SR/FST/College/2023/1423] for financial assistance in developing the infrastructure of research facilities at the Department of Nanoscience and Technology, KS Rangasamy College of Technology.

head.ce:title: Hybrid NiO nano rods-quantum carbon dots composites as a novel corrosion protective coating on EN3 mild steel in the marine environment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research endeavors to synthesize nickel oxide nanoparticles (NiO NPs) using a sonochemical process facilitated by a nitrate precursor and alkaline sodium hydroxide (NaOH). In parallel, carbon dots (CDs) were derived from lemon waste peels via a facile hydrothermal method. Subsequently, the reflux method fabricated nickel oxide- carbon dot nanocomposite (NiO/CDs) in a 5:1 ratio. The structural properties, including crystallography, crystallinity, particle size, and morphology, were comprehensively characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The synthesized nanoparticles exhibit excellent dispersion, with mean particle sizes of approximately 36.46 nm for NiO NPs and 2.17 nm for carbon dots. The carbon dots defect ratio (ID/IG) characteristics feature was estimated as 0.94 using Raman spectroscopy. NiO, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating were developed using a doctor blade method on the EN3 mild steel specimens. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy was conducted in marine water conditions with 3.5 wt% of NaCl and seawater to evaluate the stability of the nanostructured coating. Electrochemical analysis, such as the Nyquist plot, Tafel plot, and frequency response finding, demonstrated that NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating has enhanced corrosion protection in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution compares to seawater medium. These findings underscore the potential of the NiO/CDs nanostructured coating to extend the lifespan of industrial mild steel (EN3) specimens in seawater environments.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Mild steel and stainless-steel alloys are broadly used in different industries, including desalination plants, nuclear power stations, food, medical, and petroleum manufacturing, infrastructure, and transportation. Despite extensive use and versatility, steel corrosion remains a foremost issue in these sectors due to the exposure to harsh environments, including marine, chemicals, and temperature variations [1–3]. Thus, corrosion-induced steel specimen degradation causes substantial economic losses, with the subsequent expense of reconstruction requiring many resources, including material, energy, water, and workforce. Therefore, developing cost-effective protective coatings is essential to mitigate corrosion inhibition for the prolonged life of steel components [4,5].

In recent years, metal oxide nanoparticles have attracted as corrosion resistance materials such as zinc oxide (ZnO), zirconia oxide (ZrO2), copper oxide (CuO), cobalt oxide (Co3O4), magnesium oxide (MgO), titanium oxide (TiO2) including silver oxide (AgO) and lanthanum oxide (La2O3) NPs are widely studied in various corrosive atmosphere [6–13]. Among the afore-said nanoparticles, NiO NPs are one of the highly effective corrosion inhibitors and can serve as structural materials in harsh environments. The development of NiO NPs exclusive coatings has good corrosion resistance with enhanced physicochemical properties, with the requirement of low thermal conductivity, stable structural phase, reduced size effect, and high surface area [14,15]. Moreover, NiO NPs exhibit unique properties such as magnetism, mechanical robustness, and optical transparency, making them suitable for use in smart windows, supercapacitors, electrodes, catalysts, and electrochromic devices. O NPs are distinguished by their stable semiconductor properties, featuring a wide band gap from 3.6 to 4.0 eV [16,17]. It is also demonstrated as excellent durability, offers diverse manufacturing possibilities, and is an effective candidate for corrosion inhibition coatings in various industrial applications.

In the context of corrosion protection, carbon dots (CDs) also exhibit high efficiency in corrosion inhibition and scavenging of corrosive species due to their chemical stability, hydrophobic tendency, and antifouling properties. The CDs have been formed as protective barriers on steel specimens, effectively preventing biologically influenced microbial, seawater, and acidic corrosion, including CO2-saturated saline solutions [18,19]. Recently, the green synthesis of carbon dots from natural carbon source materials has attracted more than synthetic materials and methods [20,21]. Citric acid is abundantly found in lemon peels, which serve as a carbon source precursor because of its low carbonization temperature [22]. The CDs can be effectively synthesized by adopting a facial synthesis approach via a hydrothermal process. It has characteristics of quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles with diameters typically below 10 nm and exhibits exotic optical, electronic, and chemical attributes [23,24]. Additionally, it expressed a high surface area tunable surface chemistry with excellent dispersibility; hence, CDs extend substantial benefits across diverse applications, including sensing, imaging, and energy conversion [25,26].

Combining metal oxide with carbon-based material provides excellent chemical inertness and acts as a passivation layer that protects the underlying metallic surface. Adding carbon-based materials can improve the adhesion of metal oxide coatings, and their protective oxide layer remains intact and does not peel or flake off, which is crucial for long-term corrosion resistance for various steel specimens. In addition to that, carbon dots can undertake reversible redox reactions, allowing the material to repair micro-cracks or faults when exposed to corrosive circumstances [27,28]. Therefore, integrating CDs into NiO NPs has recently emerged as a promising strategy for enhancing corrosion resistance, offering both materials' synergistic properties [29]. Developing NiO/CDs nanocomposite coatings may exhibit a promising solution to mitigate corrosion challenges in the marine environment.

Salehinasab et al. investigated cathodic barrier coatings by adding NiO NPs to zinc-rich ethyl silicate at various concentrations. The study found that 3 % NiO NPs doping significantly enhanced film corrosion resistance in 3.5 % NaCl for 120 days. The composite coating was demonstrated as sacrificial anodes, improving polarization resistance up to 16,482 Ω [30]. Bahgat Radwan et al. have presented corrosion protection coatings using polystyrene nanocomposite with NiO NPs. The results indicate that NiO nanofiller enhances the wettability, surface roughness, and thermal stability of polystyrene coating. This investigation also suggests incorporating carbon-based materials extends hardness and wear resistance with low friction properties for durability coatings [31]. Dong et al. have reported that fire-resistance coating can be achieved by incorporating graphene carbon materials with bimetal hydroxide compounds. Carbon-based materials' high thermal barrier properties greatly improved coating performance [32]. Zhang et al. have recently investigated amino acid-derived carbon dots with copper inhibitors for high-performance corrosion resistance coating in sulphuric acid. After 24 h of exposure to sulphuric acid, composite coating demonstrated a corrosion inhibition efficiency of around 98.5 %. Thus, carbon materials' excellent chemical inertness characteristics and the sacrificial actions of NiO NPs have proven effective corrosion protection in diverse harsh environments [33].

Based on the literature report, the anticorrosion performances of NiO NPs with carbon dots have been investigated in limited detail. The proposed nanocomposite coating, which utilizes NiO NPs and carbon dots, may extend corrosion mitigation with the required phytochemical properties in the marine atmosphere. Hence, this study provides valuable insights into the novel green synthesized NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating for anticorrosion applications. The carbon dot CDs were prepared using natural lemon peel extract and integrated with chemically synthesized NiO NPs through reflux. The prepared CDs, NiO NPs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite were characterized by XRD, FTIR, SEM/EDX, HRTEM, and Raman spectroscopy. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) examined the corrosion behaviors for NiO/CDs nanostructured coating on mild steel specimens in the marine environment condition of 3.5 wt% NaCl and seawater. Further, the coating stability and corrosion inhibition mechanisms were systematically evaluated.

The initial precursor materials, nickel nitrate (II) hexahydrate (Ni (NO3)2·6H2O), 98 % purity and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) 98 % purity, sodium chloride (NaCl) was purchased from Loba Chemie Pvt. Ltd., Mumbai, India. Ethanol of 99.9 % purity, N-methyl pyrrolidone, and polyvinylidene fluoride were purchased from Merck. The lemon peel fruit was collected from local farms in Erode market, Tamil Nadu, India. Mild steel (MS) plates with a grade EN3 plate were purchased from local enterprises in a hardware shop in Erode, India.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of NiO NPs by sonochemical process
ce:section.ce:para: A 0.1 M solution of nickel nitrate hexahydrate was prepared by dissolving the compound in 100 mL of distilled water at 60 °C until the complete dissolution of the nitrate precursors. The pH of the solution was then adjusted to 12 by the dropwise addition of NaOH, which acted as a reducing agent. The mixture was sonicated for 2 h at 40 Hz frequency and a power of 30 % (Watts), further activating the synthesis reaction process in the precursor solution. The process ensured the homogeneous dispersion after the solution was undisturbed overnight to facilitate nucleation growth [34]. The resulting solution was washed thrice with distilled water and ethanol using centrifugation at 5000 rpm to remove impurities. The collected precipitate was dried in a hot-air oven at 65 °C for 12 h and subsequently calcined at 400 °C for 3 h in a muffle furnace, yielding black-colored NiO NPs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of quantum carbon dots from lemon peel
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, the collected lemon peels were thoroughly washed with DD water, dried, and finely ground using a domestic mixer. The powdered lemon peel was then carbonized at 400 °C for 2 h, followed by filtration to remove large impurities. Subsequently, 5 g of the carbonized powder was dispersed in 50 mL of DD water, processed in hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 12 h in an autoclave, and cooled to room temperature. The solution was centrifuged thrice at 10,000 rpm for 10 min each to eliminate residual impurities. The purified sample was then dried at 100 °C to obtain the CDs. This synthesis method underscores the utilization of natural waste materials in producing valuable nanomaterials, demonstrating a sustainable and green approach to nanotechnology.

The NiO/CDs nanocomposite was synthesized using the reflux method with a mixing ratio 5:1 for NiO NPs and CDs. Initially, 500 mg of NiO NPs and 100 mg of CDs were separately dispersed in 20 mL of distilled water and sonicated for 10 min. The sonication process plays an essential role in the synthesis and uniform dispersion of nanocomposite materials and offers efficient integration of individual components to achieve the required properties of the nanocomposite. Subsequently, both solutions were transferred in a single-neck round-bottom flask and placed on a heating mantle at 100 °C under reflux conditions for 6 h. After reflux, the solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 5000 rpm to eliminate impurities. The resulting product was dried at 100 °C for 4 h to obtain the NiO/CDs nanocomposite.

The crystallographic properties of prepared NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs composite were analyzed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD; X'Pert Pro, Netherlands). The Cu Kα (λ = 1.540 Å) was used as a radiation source at 40 kV and 30 mA, and the samples were in a range between 10° and 80°. The molecular vibrations of prepared nanopowder were examined with the help of a Fourier-Transform (IR) absorption spectrometer (Spectra 100; PerkinElmer, USA) at 400–4000 cm−1. The 100 % transmittance material, like potassium bromide (KBr), with fine mixing of nanoparticles in the ratio of 1:200, and then the pellet was prepared in a hydraulic pellet maker (Perkins Elmer, Mumbai, India). High-resolution transmission electron microscopy was employed to examine the surface morphology, crystalline nature, d spacing, and diffraction pattern using Gatan Quantum equipment (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) with an ER 965 image filter. The crystallinity phase defect and purity were investigated using Raman spectroscopy (WiTec alpha 300, Germany).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of nanostructured coating and electrochemical studies
ce:section.ce:para: The mild steel (MS) plates were initially cut into dimensions of 50 × 10 × 1 mm and polished using silica carbide paper with different micrometer grades to obtain a smooth surface. The plates were then thoroughly rinsed with DD water and acetone multiple times to remove residual debris.

Subsequently, a coating solution was formulated by blending the prepared samples (NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs) with N-methyl pyrrolidone solvent and polyvinyl fluoride binder in a ratio of 85:15:5. The mixture was homogenized to achieve a uniform dispersion. The coating solution was applied onto the MS plate using the doctor blade technique to ensure minimal thickness and uniform coverage over a 1 × 1 cm2 area. The coated plate was then dried in a hot-air oven at 60 °C for 60 min to facilitate evaporation of the solvent and binder, leaving behind a well-adhered coating. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was employed to investigate the corrosion characteristics of NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite coatings on mild steel specimens at room temperature. The experiments used a three-electrode cell setup in a 3.5 % NaCl electrolyte and seawater medium. The coated specimens served as the working electrodes (WE), platinum rods functioned as the counter electrodes (CE), and Ag/AgCl electrodes were used as the reference electrodes (RE). Linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) and potentiodynamic polarization analyses were performed to evaluate the electrochemical behavior of both uncoated and coated mild steel specimens, using a scan rate of 0.01 mV/s within a potential window of −1.8 V to 0 V. The EIS measurements were performed at open circuit potential over a frequency range from 10-5 to 10-1 Hz. These electrochemical techniques provide valuable insights into the corrosion resistance of the coatings and their effectiveness in protecting mild steel substrates from corrosion in harsh environments.

The XRD pattern of prepared NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 1 a. The predominant peaks of NiO NPs observed at the 2θ position of 37.16°, 43.27°, 62.85° 75.44° and 79.41° which correspond to crystal planes (1 1 1), (0 2 0), (0 2 2), (1 3 1), and (2 2 2), respectively. The XRD pattern confirms the cubic crystallography of NiO and is validated by JCPDS card No: 96-432-9324 [35,36]. The observed pattern of NiO NPs is indexed to the Fm3m space group (number 225) with phase parameters (a = b = c = 4.179 Å & α = β = γ = 90°). Similarly, the characteristic 2θ position of CDs is identified at 26.59° (002), and its interlayer spacing distance is 3.34Å, JCPDS card No: 26–1080 [37]. The examined pattern of CDs is reported to space group P63mc and space group number 186 with phase parameters (a = b = 2.456 Å, c = 13.392 & α = β = 90°, γ = 120°). The XRD pattern of the prepared NiO/CDs nanocomposite exhibited a perfect composition of NiO NPs and CDs without any structural deviation. However, peak intensity in nanocomposites slightly decreases, possibly due to their NiO: CDs composition in the 5:1 ratio. The observed high crystalline nature of NiO/CDs nanocomposite with reduced size effect is directly attributed to the facile sonication and reflux method approach [38].

Fig. 1b presents the FT-IR spectra of the synthesized NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposites. The broad bands measured around 3405–3441 cm−1 in all the prepared samples correspond to O–H stretching vibrations. This is because, during the preparation of the pellet with KBr and the prepared sample, either KBr or the sample absorbs moisture from the environment, leading to the appearance of the O–H functional group. Further, the peak observed in the 2922 cm−1–2926 cm−1 region corresponds to C–H stretching vibrations, which can be attributed to aliphatic or aromatic hydrocarbons belonging to the carbon dot. However, the C–H group in the NiO sample may be due to residues of nitrate precursor retained during the synthesis process [39,40].

In the spectrum of the NiO NPs, a weak and strong absorption peak, respectively, at 438 cm−1 and a strong 1026 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching mode of Ni-O vibrations [41]. Additionally, the peaks at 1385 cm−1 correspond to the bending vibration of O–H, associated with NiO NPs. The peak at 1646 cm−1 is typically connected with the bending vibration of H–O–H. These observed wave numbers for the metal oxide functional group confirmed the successful synthesis of NiO NPs. Specifically, the hydroxide functional group indicates that the NiO sample can absorb the water molecules in the surrounding environment. In the spectrum of carbon dots, the peak at 802 cm−1 is linked to the bending vibrations of C–H bonds in aromatic compounds, further confirming the complex chemical structure of CDs derived from lemon peel [42]. A peak at 1095 cm−1 signifies C–O stretching vibrations, while at 1386 cm−1, it is related to the bending vibrations of C–H bonds in aliphatic hydrocarbons within the CDs. Meanwhile, the prominent peak at 1607 cm−1 is associated with C=C stretching vibrations, whereas the peak at 1713 cm−1 corresponds to C=O stretching vibrations, highlighting the presence of carbonyl groups. The presence of organic functional strongly demonstrated the existence of carbon dots [43,44].

The FT-IR spectrum of NiO/CDs nanocomposite retains NiO and CDs with minor variations in their wavenumbers and intensity. Additionally, the disappearance of certain peaks at 438 cm−1 and 802 cm−1 suggests potential interactions between NiO and CDs. Further, hydrogen bonding or coordination between Ni ions and functional groups in CDs emerged as a new characteristic of Ni–O–C at 1033 cm−1 [44]. These findings demonstrate the successful synthesis of NiO/CDs nanocomposites, indicating that CDs effectively functionalize NiO, providing additional surface functionalities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Raman spectrum of the carbon dots
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 illustrates the Raman spectra for the synthesized carbon dots, providing valuable insights into their structural properties and crystallinity. Two characteristic peaks are observed at 1347 and 1579 cm−1, corresponding to the D (disorder) band, associated with sp3 hybridization, and the G band, associated with sp2 hybridization, respectively [45,46]. The presence of these peaks suggests the crystalline nature of the carbon dots, which is further supported by the XRD pattern analysis. The frail peak observed at the D band indicates structural defects characteristic of vitreous carbon or disordered graphite, such as dangling bonds of carbon atoms. In contrast, the sharp peak for the G band corresponds to the E2g graphite vibrational mode, indicating carbon atoms arranged in a 2D hexagonal lattice. The intensity ratio of the G band to the D band (ID/IG) is calculated as 0.94, indicating a predominance of sp2 carbons with a small fraction of sp3 carbons in the carbon dots. This purity ratio offers valuable insights into the structural composition and quality of the synthesized carbon dots.

Fig. 3 presents the HRTEM images and SAED patterns of NiO NPs, CDs, and the NiO/CDs nanocomposite. As shown in Fig. 3a, the HRTEM images reveal that the NiO NPs appear as nanorods with curved edges; each nanorod is overlapping and interconnected. The observed image is in high contrast with the high electron density, confirming the characteristic features of NiO nanorods. Furthermore, its diffraction images (Fig. 3b) highlight the high crystalline nature of the NiO NPs with an average interplanar d-spacing of 0.166 nm. The well-defined lattice fringes in the NiO NPs image reveal the presence of distinct crystallographic planes. Subsequently, the SAED pattern of NiO NPs (Fig. 3c) displays distinct and well-defined diffraction spots arranged in a ring-like pattern, indicative of the polycrystalline materials. The indexed diffraction rings correspond to the (111), (200), and (220) planes with the cubic structural phase of NiO NPs [47].

The HRTEM images of CDs (Fig. 3(d and e) exhibit ultra-fine spherical quantum dot particles, typically <10 nm. The carbon dot image shows the uniform distribution with minimal agglomeration, demonstrating highly mono-dispersed particles. It can be attributed to the mediation of the lemon peel extract during synthesis, which promotes controlled nucleation growth, resulting in quantum carbon dot formation. Using natural precursors ensures the role of stabilizing agents and surfactants that prevent aggregation, leading to homogeneous particle size distribution [48]. On the other hand, the crystalline image (Fig. 3e) reveals lattice fringes corresponding to the graphitic carbon structure, and the average d-spacing of the 0.223 nm and (002) plane is measured. The presence of these lattice fringes indicates the partial graphitization of the CDs. The SAED pattern (Fig. 3f) exhibits diffuse rings, validating the CDs' partial crystalline nature.

The HRTEM images of the NiO/CDs nanocomposite (Fig. 3 (g and h) illustrate the successful integration of CDs onto the surface of the NiO nanorods. The composite structure clearly distinguishes between the darker and lighter regions, which correspondingly represent the NiO nanorods and CDs, confirming the hybrid nature of the material. HRTEM images (Fig. 3h) reveal the lattice fringes of both components. The NiO lattice fringes remain prominent, with a d-spacing corresponding to the (200) plane, while the CDs exhibit their characteristic graphitic fringes. This observation confirms that the structural integrity of both components is preserved in the composite. The nanocomposite's SAED pattern (Fig. 3i) exhibits a combination of distinct rings and spots from NiO NPs and diffuse rings from CDs, validating the coexistence of crystalline NiO and partially crystalline CDs in the composite. The observed crystalline properties for NiO, CDs, and NiO-CDs samples are validated by the corresponding XRD pattern (Fig. 1a) and Raman spectra of carbon dots (Fig. 2).

The NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite particle sizes are 36.46 nm, 2.17 nm, and 19.8 nm, respectively. The size distribution is calculated using a histogram plot via ImageJ software by casually selecting 30 distinct particles from the TEM images [49]. The histogram particle distribution for NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs are displayed in Fig. 4 (a, c, and e), respectively. The decreased particle size in nanocomposite is attributed to the reflux process at 100 °C and the presence of CDs. This suggests that lemon peel-mediated CDs acted as a surfactant, controlling particle size growth. Fig. 4 (b, d, and f) depict EDX spectra of the prepared NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite, respectively. The elemental analysis confirms the purity of the prepared samples, with the NiO NPs showing peaks for nickel (69.33 wt%) and oxygen (30.67 wt%). On the other hand, CDs show their carbon characteristics peak at 96.87 wt%. Further, the spectrum of NiO/CDs nanocomposite reveals the presence of Ni (32.58 wt%), O (18.76 wt%), and C (48.66 wt%) with high purity. Additionally, the elemental mapping images of the NiO/CDs nanocomposite are shown in Fig. 5 , which reveals the uniform distribution of Ni, O, and C elements in the composite spectrum. This observation supports the findings obtained from the HRTEM and EDS analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrochemical characteristics of nanostructured coatings in artificial NaCl medium
ce:section.ce:para:
The potentiodynamic polarization curve in Fig. 6 (a) illustrates the corrosion behavior of MS specimens before and after coating against 3.5 wt% of NaCl electrolytes. Compared to the bare mild steel specimens, the nanostructured coatings exhibit enhanced corrosion inhibition properties. The results of the Tafel plot reveal a notable shift in the corrosion potential (Ecor
r) with more positive values for NiO (−0.984 V), CDs (−1.082 V), NiO/CDs (−1.074 V), compared to the uncoated MS plate (−0.6599 V). Additionally, the corrosion current (Icorr ) is significantly reduced for the coated samples, indicating a slower corrosion rate for the coated MS specimen. The corrosion rates for NiO-CDs, NiO, and CDs coated MS plates (0.1001, 0.2334, 0.8272 mm/year) are notably lower than that of the uncoated MS plate (1.8213 mm/year). However, these results highlight that NiO/CDs nanocomposite has a robust passivation layer, thereby mitigating NaCl ions infiltration and enhancing the durability of the metal substrate.

The observed shift in positive direction corrosion potential and decrease in corrosion current suggest improved coating stability, thus reducing the corrosion susceptibility for the coated MS specimens. The significantly lower corrosion rates indicate long-term protection afforded by the NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating, which is essential for maintaining the structural integrity of metallic components in various harsh environment applications, particularly in marine environments. Table 1 provides a comparative analysis of corrosion inhibition effectiveness for coated and uncoated MS plates using different passivation materials, emphasizing the superior performance of the NiO/CDs nanocomposite.

The Nyquist plot diagram in Fig. 6 (b) provides impedance characteristics of NiO, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanostructured coatings on MS specimens under 3.5 wt% NaCl electrolyte. The impedance spectra reveal distinct behaviors of all samples across the different frequency ranges. All samples exhibit a linear trend in the high-frequency region, indicating relatively low charge transfer resistance. However, all samples aligned toward a semicircular behavior at low-frequency regions, indicating a significant improvement in the charge transfer resistance. This phenomenon suggests that the diffusion rate of NaCl ions in the electrolyte at the coating interfaces is notably slower [50]. The extension of the semicircle radius represents the insulating characteristics of the coating, providing insight into the real-time electrochemical interaction between the coating and the electrolyte interface. Notably, the NiO/CDs sample exhibits enhanced insulating characteristics compared to NiO NPs and CD coatings, indicating superior corrosion protection performance. Meanwhile, the NiO NPs coating shows better insulating properties than CDs coating. The results highlighted the effectiveness of NiO NPs with CDs coatings in mitigating corrosion on mild steel specimens.

The electrochemical properties of the coatings are assessed using curve fitting analysis by constructing equivalent circuit diagrams [51] as an inserted image in Fig. 6 (b). Critical parameters such as charge transfer resistance (Rct), capacitance (Cdl), and polarization resistance (Rp), including impedance spectra-semi circle radius, are determined and summarized in Table 1. Notably, the NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating displayed the maximum charge transfer resistance, with a value of 13.453 Ω. The double layer capacitance (Cdl) represents the ability of the coating to store electric charge, which further mitigates the charge transfer process at a given potential, known as its dielectric nature. This higher charge transfer resistance and dielectric nature of NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating also contribute to higher polarization resistance (Rp), as shown in Table 1. These findings highlight that mild steel plates coated with NiO/CDs nanocomposites exhibit higher corrosion resistance than those coated with NiO NPs or CDs alone.

Fig. 6 (c and d) depict the logarithmic relationship between frequency (log f) Vs. Impedance magnitude (Z (Ω)) and the phase angle (α). In the high-frequency region, the impedance magnitude and its phase angle tend towards zero, which indicates that solution resistance (Rs) is very low at the interface of coating, where a rapid charge transfer process occurs, as evident by straight behaviors in the impedance spectra at high-frequency region (Fig. 6b). This process has occurred at the beginning of electrochemical reactions. As a system approaches a low-frequency region, phase angle, and impedance tend to approach higher magnitudes, making it evident that NaCl ions accumulate in the coating interface, leading to capacitive behavior [52,53]. This observation was validated by forming a semicircle curve at a high-frequency region of impedance spectra (Fig. 6 (b). This electrochemical reaction is a diffusion-limited process that controls the flow of ion mass or reacting substances towards and away from the coating surface of the MS specimen. Both frequency-dependent plots show that NiO/CDs nanocomposites have higher impedance and phase angle values, suggesting robust corrosion protection coating for mild steel plates against NaCl electrolytes.

The electrochemical corrosion characteristics of nanostructured coating on MS plate are investigated in the seawater medium, as shown in Fig. 7 , and their corresponding values are listed in Table 1. Tafel plot indicates that E (corr) and I(corr) of all coated sample have correspondingly more positive potential and high current density compared to those in 3.5 wt% NaCl medium (Fig. 6 a). Then, Nyquist plots for seawater medium, their impedance spectra reveal almost straight-line characteristics. Additionally, the reduction of Rct values in seawater medium showed that enhanced ion diffusion and charge transfer processes lead to decreased corrosion protective performance compared to the NaCl medium. Seawater electrolytes contain aggressive Cl-, Mg2+, Ca2+, SO4 2-, and HCO3– ions, which highly accelerate corrosion [54]. The seawater offers greater conductivity and higher ionic strength, with a measured pH of ∼8.2, further speeding up the charge transfer process and leading to localized corrosion. Further, the frequency-dependent Bode plot supports these findings, as decreased impedance values are demonstrated across all tested seawater coating, significantly weakening barrier properties at the coating interface and MS plate. The electrochemical performance and comparison of all the coating results are presented in Figs. 6 and 7, Table 1. Overall, NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating exhibited enhanced corrosion resistance in NaCl medium compared to seawater medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface stability of NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating
ce:section.ce:para: The SEM analysis of nanostructured coating before and after electrochemical analysis to examine the morphological changes and stability induced by NaCl ions are shown in Fig. 8 . Before corrosion, the SEM images of NiO NPs, CDs, and NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating reveal distinct surface characteristics. The NiO NPs coating displays a relatively smooth and uniform surface morphology, indicative of a homogeneous distribution of nanoparticles on the substrate. Similarly, the CD coating exhibits a uniform coverage with delicate granular structures dispersed across the surface. In contrast, the NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating displays a more complex morphology, combining nanoparticle aggregates and granular structures, suggesting a synergistic interaction between NiO NPs and CDs. After electrochemical corrosion (Fig. 8 (b, d and f), the SEM images reveal notable changes in the surface morphology; NiO NPs coating exhibits signs of corrosion, including the formation of irregular pits and cracks on the surface, localized degradation. Similarly, the CDs coating shows evidence of surface roughening. However, NiO/CDs nanocomposite coating displays a comparatively smoother surface with minimal signs of corrosion, suggesting enhanced corrosion protection conferred by the synergistic combination of NiO NPs and CDs.

By employing the sonochemical method, NiO NPs were successfully synthesized, while quantum carbon dots were green synthesized. These two components were effectively composited through the reflux method. XRD analysis provided valuable insights into the crystallinity and phase structure of the prepared nanopowder. Notably, TEM and HRTM explored the affinity of carbon dots with the active site of NiO nanorods, morphology feature and particle size, d-spacing, and crystallinity were validated. The developed anticorrosion coating comprising NiO nanorod, quantum carbon dot, NiO/CDs on EN3 mild steel specimen, where the composite coating was provided as the high level of electrochemical corrosion resistance and coating stability against infiltration of 3.5 wt% NaCl ions significantly reduced its resistance in seawater electrolytes. Further, composite coating estimated the corrosion-resistant efficiency of 94.5 % and 82.98 %, respectively, against synthetic NaCl and seawater electrolytes. The research will contribute to advancing corrosion protection methodology for mild steel in challenging marine environments.
Not applicable.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: KSG Jagan has received a B. Tech in Nanoscience and Technology from K.S. Rangasamy College of Technology. He is pursuing a M. Tech in Nanoscience and Technology. He has published research in photocatalysis, green-synthesized nanomaterials, corrosion coatings, and water treatment. His work emphasizes designing and optimizing nanostructured materials, including metal oxides, quantum dots, carbon-based nanocomposites, and hybrid nanomaterials. His contributions advance nanomaterials' structural, optical, and catalytic properties for environmental and industrial applications.

S Surendhiran is pursuing a Ph.D. in the Department of Nanoscience and Technology at K.S. Rangasamy College of Technology, Anna University, India. He has received a B. Sc and a M.Sc degree in Physics from Periyar University, India. With six years of research experience, he specializes in developing one-dimensional and two-dimensional nanostructures for energy and environmental applications. He has published research on corrosion-resistant coatings, wastewater treatment, and supercapacitor applications. He possesses extensive expertise in operating and interpreting results from advanced analytical instruments, including electrochemical workstations, XPS, FTIR, TG/DTA, UV-Vis, and PL spectroscopy.

S Savitha has received a B. Tech and an M. Tech degree in Nanoscience and Technology from K.S. Rangasamy College of Technology. She is currently pursuing a Ph.D. in wastewater treatment. Her research focuses on nanomaterials for environmental and energy applications, particularly emphasising wastewater treatment, corrosion prevention, and nanocomposites. Additionally, her work involves developing advanced nanomaterials for efficient water treatment and exploring their potential in biological applications, particularly tissue engineering.

T.M. Naren Vidaarth has completed his B. Tech and is pursuing an M. Tech at the Center for Nanoscience and Technology, K.S. Rangasamy College of Technology, Tiruchengode. His research expertise lies in synthesizing nanomaterials through various green and chemical synthesis methods and characterizing them through multiple physicochemical techniques. His research primarily focuses on water treatment, corrosion resistance, energy storage, and antimicrobial applications. Naren has also published several articles in these fields in Scopus-indexed journals, demonstrating a strong interest in nanoscience and technology

A. Karthik received his Ph.D. in Technology (Nano) from Anna University, Chennai. He holds an M. Tech in Nano Science and Technology, as well as an M. Sc and B. Sc in Physics. He is a recipient of the CSIR Senior Research Fellowship (SRF), Delhi. Additionally, he has 12 years of research experience, including 8 years of teaching at K.S. Rangasamy College of Technology, Tamil Nadu, India. He has also served as a guest faculty member at the Central University of Pondicherry and the Central University of Tamil Nadu. He is actively involved in the mass production of various nanometal oxides, including green catalytic materials, for diverse societal applications such as wastewater and greywater treatment, environmental corrosion protection, thermal barrier coatings, electrode materials for supercapacitors, and biomaterials for tissue engineering.

N. Lenin holds a Ph.D. in Nanotechnology from Anna University, Tamil Nadu, India. He has an academic background with an M.Sc. in Physics, an M.Tech. in Nano Science and Technology, and a Ph.D. in Technology, with over 30 international peer-reviewed publications. Currently, he is an Assistant Professor in the Department of Aeronautical Engineering at Park College of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore, India. His research focuses on the synthesis and characterization of nanomaterials, nano ferrites, and polymers using various techniques. Additionally, he is involved in research on reducing electromagnetic radiation (EMR) interference from telecommunication towers, ships, and aircraft. His interdisciplinary contributions advance materials science and engineering applications

R. Senthilmurugan is a Professor in the Department of Mechatronics Engineering at K.S. Rangasamy College of Technology, India. With over two decades of experience in teaching and industry, he earned his Ph.D. in Mechanical Engineering from Anna University in 2018. He holds a B.E. in Mechanical Engineering and an M.E. in Computer-Aided Design, both from Anna University. He has 12 years of teaching experience, including advanced courses and laboratories. His industrial background spans design, production, and consultancy, contributing to pneumatic clamps, nanocoatings, and automation systems. He has published extensively on nanocomposites, quality improvement, and photovoltaic systems and holds a patent for a nanocomposite polymeric film for vehicle exhaust. He is a life member of ISTE and has led workshops on emerging technologies
